revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Himetataraisuzu-hime]][[fa:هیمه‌تاتاراایسوزو-هیمه]][[fr:Himetataraisuzu-hime]][[ja:ヒメタタライスズヒメ]][[ko:히메타타라이스즈히메]][[simple:Himetataraisuzu-hime]][[th:ธิดาฮิเมตาตาไรซูซุ]][[zh:媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命]]
{{Infobox person|image=File:Empress-Hime-tatara-RA.jpg|gender={{ill|female|qid=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|qid=Q595038|1=Empress of Japan}}|spouse={{ill|Jimmu|qid=Q200188|1=Emperor Jimmu}}; {{ill|Tagishimimi|qid=Q11075750|1=Tagishimimi}}|children={{ill|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|1=Emperor Suizei}}; {{ill|Hikoyai-no-mikoto|qid=Q11086120|1=Hikoyai}}; {{ill|Kamuyaimimi-no-mikoto|qid=Q11588756|1=Kamuyaimimi}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|qid=Q8009611|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Ōmononushi|qid=Q10936701|1=Ōmononushi}}|mother={{ill|Tamakushi-hime|qid=Q11572681|1=Tamakushi-hime}}|relatives={{ill|Isuzu-yori Hime-no-mikoto|qid=Q10881557|1=Isuzuyori-hime}}; {{ill|Kamo no Kimi|qid=Q55809532|1=Kamo no Okimi}}}}

{{Short description|Legendary first empress of Japan}}
{{Rough translation|1=ja|listed=yes|date=January 2023}}
{{Cleanup|date=May 2022|reason=Originally created via machine translation.  Names and grammar need fixing.  Meaning obscured in places by the MT engine getting things wrong.  Refer to source article at [[:ja:ヒメタタライスズヒメ]] for reference, as some of this EN article is impossible to understand correctly without viewing the JA source.}}

{{Infobox royalty
| consort      = yes
| name         = Himetataraisuzu-hime
| image        = 神武天皇 媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛命 emperor Jimmu and Himetataraisuzuhime no mikoto 日本国開闢由来記1.jpg
| caption      = Emperor Jimmu and Himetataraisuzu-hime from the first volume of [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]'s "Nihon kokusei rya ki".
| succession   = {{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|qid=Q595038|lt=Empress of Japan|1=Empress of Japan}}
| reign        = 660–581 BC
| succession1  = [[:en:Empress dowager of Japan|Empress dowager of Japan]]
| reign1       = appointed in 581 BC
| spouse       = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}
| issue        = {{ubl|[[Hikoyai]]|[[Kamuyaimimi]]|{{ill|Emperor Suizei|en|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|lt=Emperor Suizei|1=Emperor Suizei}}}}
| father       = {{ubl|[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'')|[[Ōmononushi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')}}
| mother       = {{ubl|[[Tamakushi-hime]] (''Nihon Shoki'')|[[:en:Seyadatara-hime|Seyadatara-hime]] (''Kojiki'')}}
| religion     = [[Shinto]]
}}
'''Himetataraisuzu-hime''' {{Nihongo|2=媛蹈鞴五十鈴媛}}<ref name="Nipponica">{{Cite web|last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及|first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus,精選版 日本国語大辞典,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,百科事典マイペディア,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典|title=神武天皇とは|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E6%AD%A6%E5%A4%A9%E7%9A%87-82633|access-date=2022-01-13|website=コトバンク|language=ja}}</ref><ref name="japanese-god-name-dictionary-2">"Japanese God Name Dictionary" p320 "Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"

『日本神名辞典』p320「比売多多良伊須気余理比売」</ref>{{efn|
She is referred to by several different names and several different spellings. Among them [[:en:Isukeyori-hime|Isukeyori-hime]].<ref name="digital-museum-2023a">{{cite web |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9390 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Digital Museum |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230317221441/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9390 |archive-date=2023-03-17 |access-date=13 November 2023}}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia-of-shinto-2015a" />
}} is a Japanese goddess, a mythological figure in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Chronicles of Japan), the first {{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|qid=Q595038|lt=Empress of Japan|1=Empress of Japan}}, and the first wife of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}.<ref name="encyclopedia-of-shinto-2015a">{{Cite web |date=2015-06-05 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Isukeyorihime |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=77 |access-date=2023-03-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150605211825/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=77 |archive-date=2015-06-05}}</ref>

Although details vary, her parents are described as an influential {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato Province}} woman and a [[kami]]. She is said to have married {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|qid=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}} Jimmu and given birth to the second emperor, {{ill|Emperor Suizei|en|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|lt=Emperor Suizei|1=Emperor Suizei}}.<ref name="歴代天皇Shoki-神武">Illustrated Chronicle of the Emperors of Japan, p.37-41, "Emperor Jimmu".</ref><ref name="japanese-god-name-dictionary-2" />

== Depiction by ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'' ==

=== Birth ===

According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', when Kamuyamato-Iwarebiko (also known as {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}) was searching for a wife, he was told about a woman named {{nihongo|[[Tamakushi-hime|Seyadatarahime]]|勢夜陀多良比売}} who bore a daughter after she was impregnated by [[Ōmononushi]]. Ōmononushi had taken the form of a red arrow and struck Seyadatara-hime's genitals while she was [[:en:Defecating|Defecating]] in a ditch. Iwarebiko wooed this daughter, named {{nihongo|Hototatara-Isusukihime|富登多多良伊須須岐比売}} (also known as {{nihongo|Himetatara-Isukeyorihime|比売多多良伊須気余理比売}}, an altered form of the name which omits the [[taboo]] word {{nihongo|hoto|ホト|"genitals"}}), and took her as his wife.<ref name="Kojiki">{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=115–117}}</ref><ref name="Ponsonby-Fane2014">{{cite book |author=R. A. B. Ponsonby-Fane |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UVO4AwAAQBAJ&pg=PT412 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=3 June 2014 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-136-89301-8 |page=412}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Kadoya |first1=Atsushi |title=Ōmononushi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=126 |accessdate=22 September 2015 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia-of-shinto-2015a" />

Like the ''Kojiki'', the main narrative of the first volume of the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' first describes Himetataraisuzu-hime as the offspring of the god of Ōmononushi. However, the ''Nihon Shoki'' also contains an alternative story which portrays her as the child of the god {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]|事代主神}} and the goddess {{nihongo|[[:en:Mizokuhihime|Mizokuhihime]]|溝樴姫}} - also known as {{nihongo|Tamakushihime|玉櫛姫}} - conceived after Kotoshironushi transformed himself into a gigantic ''{{ill|Wani (dragon)|en|Wani (dragon)|qid=Q20049265|lt=wani|1=Wani (dragon)}}'' and had sex with her.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=61–62|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref> Likewise, the main narrative in the third and fourth volumes of ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to her as the daughter of Kotoshironushi rather than Ōmononushi.<ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=132|chapter=Book III|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref><ref>{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspage=138|chapter=Book IV|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}</ref>

Her house was located on the Sai River and near Sai-jinja Shrine, near {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}.<ref name="An Encounter on the Sai River" />

=== Marriage with Emperor Jimmu ===
According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and other sources, Iwarehiko (later Emperor Jimmu) left the ''Land of Himuka'' and made an expedition to the east, and after many battles, established his government in the {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato region|1=Yamato Province}}. Iwarehiko built the Palace of Kashiwara in modern-day {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|qid=Q496319|lt=Kashihara|1=Kashihara, Nara}} at the foot of {{ill|Mount Unebi|en|Mount Unebi|qid=Q17606448|lt=Mount Unebi|1=Mount Unebi}} and ascended to the throne as the first emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus,精選版 日本国語大辞典,旺文社日本史事典 三訂版,百科事典マイペディア,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典 |title=神武天皇とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E6%AD%A6%E5%A4%A9%E7%9A%87-82633 |access-date=2022-05-07 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>

Prior to his accession to the throne, Iwarehiko needed to have a {{ill|Queen consort|en|Queen consort|qid=Q719039|lt=consort|1=Queen consort}} worthy of being the first Empress. Okume-no-mikoto, a vassal of Iwarehiko, suggested Himetataraisuzu-hime as a candidate for his wife.<ref name="読み解き事典">Tomitatara Isukihime, Hiyotara Isukiyorihime and Ehime-Tobuttoro Isuzuhime in "Nihon no Kami Yomikaku Jiten", p199-200</ref><ref name="学研20152" /> According to the ''Kojiki'', Okume-no-mikoto explains the story of Himetataraisuzu-hime's birth to Iwarehiko and tells him that she deserved to be his rightful wife.<ref name="ヒメたち98" /><ref name="学研20152" /> In the ''Kojiki'', there is another story in which Iwarehiko and Okume-no-mikoto witnessed seven women on the shore of the Sai River and selected a wife from among them.<ref name="An Encounter on the Sai River">{{Cite web |title=An Encounter on the Sai River  |url=https://www3.pref.nara.jp/miryoku/narakikimanyo/manabu/story/story07/ |access-date=2023-03-04 |website=www3.pref.nara.jp}}</ref>

Himetataraisuzu-hime had a notable poetic exchange with them, and then Jimmu stayed the night at her house.<ref name="An Encounter on the Sai River" />

According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', their marriage took place on September 24 of the year before his accession.{{efn|In ''Nihon Shoki'', he began his search for a consort on August 16th by the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]], the year before his accession to the throne ("The New Moon of August, [[Yang Earth Dragon|Earth Dragon (Chinese Zodiac)]]"),<ref name="読み解き事典" /><ref name="歴代皇后-神武">"Overview of Empress Characters", p26-27 "The First Emperor Jimmu, Empress Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"</ref> and he chose Himetataraisuhime as her consort and married her on September 24 by the lunar calendar ("September New Moon").<ref name="新撰大人名辞典">Japanese Name Dictionary (Shin-Sen-Otona-Name Jiten), p.262 "Himetataraisuhime no Mikoto</ref><ref name="女性人名辞典">"Japanese Women's Name Dictionary Popular Edition" p876 "Himetataraisuzu Himetataraisuzu"</ref>}} Himetataraisuzu-hime became Empress when Emperor Jimmu ascended the throne the following year.{{efn|In Nihon Shoki, the year of Emperor Jimmu's accession to the throne is taken as the year of the [[:en:Metal Rooster|Metal Rooster]]. According to the calculation method established in the Meiji era (1868-1912), taking into account the Chinese prophecy theory and the [[Shin-Yu Revolution theory]], this year is {{ill|660 BC|en|660 BC|qid=Q1059526|lt=660 BC|1=660 BC}}. In the past, this was considered a historical fact, but in modern times it is not usually considered a historical fact.<ref name="歴代天皇Shoki-神武"/> For more information, see {{ill|Japanese imperial year|en|Japanese imperial year|qid=Q1209728|lt=Japanese imperial year|1=Japanese imperial year}}.}}

=== After the death of Emperor Jimmu ===
According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Jimmu died at the age of 127. Although there are differences in details, the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' describe a succession struggle that occurred among his children after his death.

Before he left for the eastern expedition from the "Land of Himuka", Iwarehiko had married Ohiratsuhime and they had a child.{{efn|The "Kojiki" records the names of two of his children. {{ill|Tagishimimi|en|Tagishimimi|qid=Q11075750|lt=Tagishimimi|1=Tagishimimi}} (<ref name="日本神名辞典-多芸">Nihon Shinmei Jiten (Dictionary of the Japanese Gods), p235 "Tagishimimikoto".</ref>) and [[Kisumimi]] (<ref name="日本神名辞典-岐須">Nihon Shinmei Jiten (Dictionary of the Divine Names of Japan), p151 "Kisumimi-no-mikoto".</ref>).<ref name="読み解き事典-阿比良">Nihon no Kami Yomikaku Jiten (A Dictionary of Japanese Gods and Goddesses), p.40-41 "Abira Hime / Ohira Tsuhime</ref><ref name="歴代天皇Shoki-綏靖">Illustrated Chronicle of the Emperors of Japan, p.42-43, "Emperor Suizei</ref> On the other hand, the Nihongo Shoki has only the name of {{ill|Tagishimimi|en|Tagishimimi|qid=Q11075750|lt=Tegenmimi-no-mikoto|1=Tagishimimi}} (Tagishimimino-mikoto) and does not mention the name of the person corresponding to Gisumimi-no-mikoto.<ref name="読み解き事典-阿比良"/>}} However, these children were reduced to the status of [[:en:Bastard child|bastards]] when Iwarehiko made Himetataraisuzu-hime the rightful Empress. When Emperor Jimmu died, his bastard son, Tagishimi, wanted to succeed to the throne himself.{{efn|According to Nihon Shoki and other sources, Tagishimi served under Emperor Jimmu for a long time. According to Nihon Shoki and other sources, Tagishimi served under Emperor Jimmu for a long time, but he was described as having a difficult personality and a tendency to "disobey humanity".<ref name="歴代天皇Shoki-綏靖"/> These portrayals are not necessarily considered to be the true historical facts. In the replacement of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} with {{ill|Emperor Suizei|en|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|lt=Emperor Suizei|1=Emperor Suizei}}, the {{ill|ultimogeniture|en|ultimogeniture|qid=Q2568955|lt=ultimogeniture|1=Ultimogeniture}} took place, which is thought to have been common in ancient Japan. However, later on {{ill|Primogeniture|en|Primogeniture|qid=Q250843|lt=Primogeniture|1=Primogeniture}} became common, and some interpret this to mean that the explanation that the elder brother was eliminated because he was a bad person was necessary for readers who consider first-born inheritance to be legitimate.<ref name="歴代天皇Shoki-綏靖"/>}}

In the ''Kojiki'', Tagishimi took the widowed Empress Himetataraisuzu-hime as his wife and tried to assassinate the legitimate children of Emperor Jimmu and Empress Himetataraisuzu-hime. Aware of his plans, Himetataraisuzu-hime wrote two poems to her children to warn them of the danger.<ref name="ヒメたち98">Hime in Mythology: The Other Kojiki, p98-101 "The First Empress Who Saved Her Son with a Song".</ref>{{Refn|In the ''Kojiki'', "天皇崩後、其庶兄當藝志美美命、娶其嫡后伊須気持余理比売之時、將殺其三弟而謀之間、其御祖伊須気持余理比売之患苦而、以歌令知其御子等"|group="Kojiki"}}

Learning of the plot from their mother's poems, the legitimate sons attacked Tagishimi first and defeated him. Kannuma Kawamimi-no-mikoto, who played the most active role in the attack, succeeded his father and ascended to the throne as the second emperor, {{ill|Emperor Suizei|en|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|lt=Emperor Suizei|1=Emperor Suizei}}. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Himetataraisuzu-hime took the title of "[[:en:Empress Dowager|Empress Dowager]]".

{{ill|Emperor Suizei|en|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|lt=Emperor Suizei|1=Emperor Suizei}} took Princess [[Isuzuyori-hime]] (五十鈴依媛命) as his {{ill|Queen consort|en|Queen consort|qid=Q719039|lt=consort|1=Queen consort}}. Isuzuyori-hime was Himetataraisuzu-hime's younger sister and {{ill|Emperor Suizei|en|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|lt=Emperor Suizei|1=Emperor Suizei}}'s aunt. Other versions of this story claim that Kawamata Biyori or Itoorihime became {{ill|Emperor Suizei|en|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|lt=Emperor Suizei's|1=Emperor Suizei}} consort.

=== Children ===
Based on the ''Nihon Shoki'' and the ''Kojiki'', Himetataraisuzu-hime and Emperor Jimmu had three children: [[Hikoyai]] (日子八井命), [[Kamuyaimimi]] (神八井耳命), and {{ill|Emperor Suizei|en|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|lt=Emperor Suizei|1=Emperor Suizei}}. Hikoyai is only mentioned in the ''Kojiki'', whereas the other two children are mentioned in both texts. Kamuyaimimi became the founder of the {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|qid=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan|1=Ō clan}}.

{{A genealogical tree based on the Kojiki}}

=== Siblings ===
Himetataraisuzu-hime's mother gave birth to two other children:
* [[Isuzuyori-hime]] -  Himetataraisuzu-hime's sister. She became the Empress of the second emperor, {{ill|Emperor Suizei|en|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|lt=Emperor Suizei|1=Emperor Suizei}}.<ref name="神話の中のヒメたち-もうひとつの古事記-2" /><ref>『図説　歴代天皇紀』p42-43「綏靖天皇」</ref>
* [[Kamo no Okimi]] - Himetataraisuzu-hime's older brother. In the ''[[Kujiki]]'', he served {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} and became ''Shinshoku Kokusei Tayu''.<ref name="神話の中のヒメたち-もうひとつの古事記-2">『神話の中のヒメたち　もうひとつの古事記』p94-97「初代皇后は「神の御子」」</ref>

== Theories regarding lineage ==
{{Incomprehensible|date=March 2023|sect=section}}
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and the ''[[Kojiki]]'', although the details of the stories differ, Himetataraisuzu-hime is depicted as having a mother who is "the daughter of an influential person (a deity)" and a father who is "a deity." There is a theory that Emperor Jimmu, the first emperor of Japan, may have married a Himetataraisuzu-hime, a "daughter of a god," in order to legitimize his regime.

It is said that the mother's maternal family belongs to Mishima of Settsu (Osaka Prefecture) and her paternal family belongs to Miwa of Yamato (Nara Prefecture). It is theorized that this suggests cooperation between several powerful clans in the Kansai region,{{efn|If Himetataraisuhime's father is [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] or [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]], as ''Nihon Shoki'' suggests, then Himetataraisuhime has roots in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}} as well as in the Kinki region.<ref name="ヒメたち94"/>}} and that the marriage of Iwarehiko, a foreigner from Himuka (Hyūga Province), was supported by a powerful person from Kawachi Province (Yamato and Settsu Provinces).{{efn|Strictly speaking, it was in ancient times that {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|qid=Q860290|lt=Provinces of Japan|1=Provinces of Japan}} such as Yamato and Settsu were established.}} There is also a theory that this shows Iwarehiko not only intended to conquer by military force, but also consolidate his control in the countryside by spreading his influence. There is also an interpretation that it indicates that Emperor Jimmu's forces secured iron manufacturing technology.

=== Grandfather: Mizokuhi of Mishima ===
In the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan), the mother is said to be the daughter of '''Mishima Mizokuhi''', although there are some differences in wording. "Mizokuhiis also written as 溝樴, 溝樴耳神, and 溝杙 in the ''Kojiki'' (Records of Ancient Matters), and as 湟咋, 溝杭 ({{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|qid=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Seijiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}), Mizokui ({{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|qid=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Seijiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}), and other characters may also be applied. "In addition, there are some historical records that refer to him as Mimi-gami, suggesting that he was an object of worship as a divine being. In the genealogy of the Kamo clan, this deity is said to be the ancestor of the [[Kamo clan]] and the Katsuragi Kunizo. There is also a theory that the name of the deity is related to "Mishima" and that it is the same deity as [[Sukunabikona|Shogikona]], who was the ancestor deity of the Mishima prefectures.

The place name "Mishima" is thought to be {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}} {{ill|Mishima District, Osaka|en|Mishima District, Osaka|qid=Q783553|lt=Mishima County|1=Mishima District, Osaka}} (present-day northern {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}). The [[Engishiki]] (927) lists Mishima Kamojinshya (Mishima-e, {{ill|Takatsuki|en|Takatsuki|qid=Q335423|lt=Takatsuki City|1=Takatsuki}}) and Mizokuijinshya (Ibaraki, Osaka|Ibaraki City), suggesting that "Mizokuhi of Mishima" was worshipped in this area.{{efn|{{ill|Mizokui Shrine|ja|溝咋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizokui Shrine|1=Mizokui Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11564491}} refers to Mishima Mizokui (Mishima Mizokui) as the founder of the shrine<ref name="読み解き事典"/> and says that the Mishima clan would have been a powerful clan in the ancient Kawachi region.<ref name="ヒメたち94">Hime in Mythology: Another Kojiki, p94-97 "The First Empress is the Son of God".</ref>}}

In the Edo period, {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Nencho Motoi|1=Motoori Norinaga}} interpreted this "mizo (groove)" to refer to a {{ill|Toilet (room)|en|Toilet (room)|qid=Q7813355|lt=toilet|1=Toilet (room)}} built over a stream of water, and this has become a common theory. Eiichi Mitani and others have adopted this theory, and there is also a theory that the toilet is strongly related to birth rituals. Kazuo Higo (Professor Emeritus, Tokyo University of Education) disagreed, saying that "mizo" means a ditch in a paddy field. Masayuki Tsugita developed this theory, claiming that Mishima-gun was an ideal place for rice cultivation and that "Mizokuhi of Mishima" was a farming god.

=== Mothers: Tamakushihime and Seyadatara-hime ===
Her mother's name is Tamakushihime in the ''Nihon Shoki'' (Chronicles of Japan) and Seyadatara-hime in the ''Kojiki'' (Records of Ancient Matters). Both are said to have been known as beautiful women.{{Refn|In the ''Nihon Shoki'', "是国色之秀者".|group="Shoki"}}{{Refn|In the ''Kojiki'', "其容姿麗美".|group="Kojiki"}}

{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Noninaga Motoi|1=Motoori Norinaga}} compares Seiya to {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} Hiragun-gun, Seinomura ({{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}} {{ill|Ikoma District, Nara|en|Ikoma District, Nara|qid=Q971003|lt=Ikoma County|1=Ikoma District, Nara}} [[Sangō, Nara|Misato Town]]).

=== Anecdotes of her birth in ''Kojiki'' ===
In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', she is said to be the daughter of [[Ōmononushi|Omononushi]].{{efn|Omonoshu (大物主神) was originally the [[:en:Clan deity|Clan deity]] of the {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}.<ref name="ニッポニカ-大物主">The {{ill|Encyclopedia Nipponica|en|Encyclopedia Nipponica|qid=Q2660112|lt=Encyclopedia Nipponica|1=Encyclopedia Nipponica}}, [[Shogakukan]], 1984-1994, "Omonoshigami" [https://kotobank.jp/word/大物主神-450980 cotbank version], viewed 30 July 2018</ref> On the other hand, Omononushi may be another name for [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] (a descendant of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]), and in Nihongo Shoki, Omononushi is the {{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|qid=Q495568|lt=Mitama|1=Mitama}} of Okuninushi.<ref name="ニッポニカ-大物主"/> Both were originally considered to be different deities.<ref name="大辞林-大物主">[[Daijirin|Daijirin Third Edition]], {{ill|Sanseidō|en|Sanseidō|qid=Q1058813|lt=Sanseidō|1=Sanseidō}}, "Omonoshūjin" [https://kotobank.jp/word/大物主神-450980 Cotbank version], viewed 30 July 2018.</ref><ref name="朝日人物-大物主">Asanichi Nihon Rekishi Jinbun Jiten (Encyclopedia of Japanese Historical Figures), {{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|qid=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun|1=The Asahi Shimbun}}, 1994, "Omonoshigami" [https://kotobank.jp/word/大物主神-450980 Cotobank edition], viewed 30 July 2018.</ref>}} His birthplace is {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} in the Yamato region.

She is said to have originally been named '''Hottataraisukihime'''. It is also said that she was a beautiful woman like her mother.

However, she disliked the word "hoto" and changed her name to '''Himetataraisuzu-hime'''.

The dropping of the "hoto" may be related to the word being linked to genitals.<ref name="digital-museum-2023a" />

== Relation to Tatara Iron Manufacturing==
There is a theory that the "Tatara" part of the name Himetataraisuzu-hime is interpreted in connection with {{ill|Tatara (furnace)|en|Tatara (furnace)|qid=Q3131218|lt=Tatara iron manufacturing|1=Tatara (furnace)}}, indicating iron manufacturing in ancient Japan.<ref name="鈴本1979"/><ref name="小路田2005"/><ref name="学研20152">『古事記と日本の神々がわかる本』p90-91「イスケヨリヒメの物語」</ref>{{efn|The character "鞴" used in Nihon Shoki refers to the {{ill|Bellows|en|Bellows|qid=Q208103|lt=Bellows|1=Bellows}} used in iron making.<ref name="小路田2005">{{ill|Yasunao Kojita|ja|小路田泰直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasunao Kojita|1=Yasunao Kojita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11463830}} ({{ill|Nara Women's University|en|Nara Women's University|qid=Q1148082|lt=Nara Women's University|1=Nara Women's University}}, History),[http://nwudir.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10935/433/1/20080522_2.pdf "The 'Kojiki' and 'Nihonshoki'. Narrating the History of the Formation of the Japanese State : From the Perspective of Fire and Iron"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180730170613/http://nwudir.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10935/433/1/20080522_2.pdf |date=2018-07-30 }}, 2005 (Methods in Japanese History, No. 2 pp. 145-168), viewed 30 July 2018.</ref>}}{{efn|In modern Japan (before the gathering of the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}), the origins of iron manufacturing in Japan have been traced back to the Kamishiro period,<ref name="鈴本1979">Teiichi Suzumoto ({{ill|Chemical Society of Japan|en|Chemical Society of Japan|qid=Q926062|lt=Chemical Society of Japan|1=Chemical Society of Japan}}),[https://doi.org/10.20665/kagakukyouiku.27.1_24 "Tatara Iron and Wako Memorial Hall"], 1979 Journal of Chemical Education, 1979, vol. 27, no. 1, 24-27, {{doi|10.20665/kagakukyouiku.27.1_24}}, viewed 30 July 2018.</ref> with episodes in the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki stating that when [[Amaterasu]] hid in [[Amano-Iwato]], iron from "Amakayama (Nihon Shoki)" or "Amakinzan (Kojiki)" was used for metalworking. (Nihon Shoki)" or "Amakinzan (Kojiki)".<ref name="鈴本1979"/><ref name="進藤1975">Yoshihiko Shindo (Institute for Asian Studies, {{ill|Asia University (Japan)|en|Asia University (Japan)|qid=Q4402470|lt=Asia University|1=Asia University (Japan)}}),[http://id.nii.ac.jp/1385/00013673/ "A Study on the Origin of Ironware Culture in Ancient Japan"], 1975 Journal of the College of Liberal Arts, Asia University, 12, 99-118, viewed 30 July 2018.</ref> In modern times, it is generally accepted that iron making technology was introduced from mainland China along with rice cultivation,<ref name="進藤1975"/> but archaeological evidence is insufficient,<ref name="進藤1975"/> and no definitive theory has been established about its origin or age.<ref name="鈴本1979"/> In terms of literary sources, iron manufacturing is specifically detailed in the {{ill|Izumono Kuni Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumono Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the 8th century, and it is thought that iron manufacturing was already established in this period.<ref name="鈴本1979"/>}}{{efn|Teiichi Suzumoto ({{ill|Chemical Society of Japan|en|Chemical Society of Japan|qid=Q926062|lt=Chemical Society of Japan|1=Chemical Society of Japan}}) has argued that the huge [[Emperor Nintoku#Nintoku's tomb|Emperor Nintoku's Mausoleum]], which dates from around the early 5th century, was probably made possible to build by the establishment of iron tools, and that the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Imperial Court|1=Yamato Kingship}} of the time probably had secured {{ill|Tatara (furnace)|en|Tatara (furnace)|qid=Q3131218|lt=tatara iron|1=Tatara (furnace)}} technology.<ref name="鈴本1979"/> Fuigo has been excavated from the {{ill|Higashi Nara Ruins|ja|東奈良遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Nara Ruins|1=Higashi Nara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11526573}} (in {{ill|Ibaraki, Osaka|en|Ibaraki, Osaka|qid=Q502403|lt=Ibaraki City|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}), and some have linked this to iron manufacturing by the Yamato Court.<ref name="吉野1975">Hiroshi Yoshino,[https://doi.org/10.20620/nihonbungaku.24.8_75 "The Story of Tara and Ota Taneko (Qicaian Zoso III)"], Japanese Literature, 1975, vol. 24, no. 8, p. 75-83, {{doi|10.20620/nihonbungaku.24.8 _75}}, viewed on July 30, 2018.</ref> At this Higashinara site (discovered in 1971), {{ill|Dōtaku|en|Dōtaku|qid=Q2312868|lt=Dōtaku|1=Dōtaku}} and their casts have been excavated, and it is certain that bronze bells were made there.<ref name="ブリタニカ-東奈良遺跡">''{{ill|Britannica International Encyclopedia|en|Britannica International Encyclopedia|qid=Q109359966|lt=Britannica International Encyclopedia of Minor Subjects|1=Britannica International Encyclopedia}}'', 2014, "East Nara Site" [https://kotobank.jp/word/東奈良遺跡-119134 cotbank edition], viewed 30 July 2018.</ref>}}

According to {{ill|Yasunao Kojita|ja|小路田泰直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasunao Kojita|1=Yasunao Kojita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11463830}} ({{ill|Nara Women's University|en|Nara Women's University|qid=Q1148082|lt=Nara Women's University|1=Nara Women's University}}), 'Tatara' refers to a Tatara furnace, and "Hoto" refers to the pubic area as well as the fireplace.<ref name="yasunao-kojita-2005a">Yasunao Kojita (Nara Women's University, History), "[http://nwudir.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10935/433/1/20080522_2.pdf History of the formation of the Japanese nation as told by" Kojiki "and" Nihon Shoki ": From the perspective of fire and iron"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180730170613/http://nwudir.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10935/433/1/20080522_2.pdf |date=2018-07-30 }} (PDF), 2005 ("Method of Japanese History" No. No. 2 pp.145-168), viewed July 30, 2018.</ref>{{efn|The word "hoto" (溶鉱炉) also refers to "blast furnace".<ref name="吉野1975"/>}} In other words, the fact that Emperor Jimmu took Himetataraisuzu-hime (= HimetataraHimetataraisuzu-hime = Hotataraisukihime) as his wife is interpreted as an indication that the royal family controlled the iron and steel industry.<ref name="yasunao-kojita-2005a" /> Yutaka Yoshino (Japan Literature Association) states that the name "HotataraHimetataraisuzu-hime" refers to a priestess who served the god of molten ore and the blast furnace.<ref name="裕-1975a">{{Cite journal |last=裕 |first=吉野 |date=1975 |title=タタラと大田田根子の話(其蜩庵雑草 Iii) |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/nihonbungaku/24/8/24_KJ00010006430/_article/-char/ja/ |journal=日本文学 |volume=24 |issue=8 |pages=75–83 |doi=10.20620/nihonbungaku.24.8_75}}</ref>

{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Nomoninaga Motoi|1=Motoori Norinaga}} and other early modern {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} scholars did not interpret the word "Tatara" in Himetataraisuzu-hime to mean a bellows.<ref name="裕-1975a" /> In their view, the word "Tatara" is a slang term used by blacksmiths and is dismissed from its ties to steelmaking as it is unsuitable for the name of a noble empress.<ref name="裕-1975a" /> "Some interpret "Tatara" as a derivative of "stand," meaning "stood up (surprised by an arrow in the pubic region)" or "had an arrow put up (in the pubic region).<ref>『神道大辞典（縮刷版）』p1227「ヒメタタライスズヒメノミコト」</ref><ref name="japanese-god-name-dictionary-2" />

== Objects of faith==
{{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} founded the [[Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Jingu]] in 1890, where Emperor Jimmu and Himetatharaisuhime are enshrined as the main deities.<ref name="神道大辞典">Shinto Daijiten (reduced edition), p1227 "Himetataraisuhime no Mikoto".</ref><ref name="日本神名辞典-比売">Nihon Shinmei Jiten (Dictionary of the Japanese Gods), p320 "Hiyotara Isuke Yorihime".</ref>。

Himetataraisuzu-hime also came to be revered as a "{{Nihongo|2=子守明神|3=Komori Myōjin}}" because she saved children, and is enshrined as the main deity at {{ill|Isagawa Shrine|ja|率川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isagawa Shrine|qq=}} (Honkomori-cho, Nara City, Nara Prefecture).<ref name="神話の中のヒメたち-もうひとつの古事記-3">『神話の中のヒメたち　もうひとつの古事記』p98-101「歌で御子救った初代皇后」」</ref>{{efn|The {{ill|Isagawa Shrine|ja|率川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isagawa Shrine|qq=}} is a regent shrine of the Miwasan and {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}, which is said to be the hometown of Himetataraisuhime.<ref name="ヒメたち98"/>}} In June of every year, the Nitsukawa Shrine holds the {{Nihongo|"Saegusa Festival" (commonly known as the lily festival)|「三枝祭」（通称：ゆり祭り）|`Saigusasai'(tsūshō: Yuri matsuri)}}, where Himetataraisuzu-hime is worshipped by offering lilies grown at {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}.<ref name="神話の中のヒメたち-もうひとつの古事記-3" />

At the upper reaches of the {{Nihongo|Sakai River|狭井川}}, where Himetataraisuzu-hime's parents lived, there is the {{Nihongo|Sakai Shrine|狭井神社}}. Here, the main deity is Himetataraisuzu-hime, but also [[Ōmononushi|Omononushi]] (father of Himetataraisuzu-hime according to the ''Kojiki''), Seyadatara-hime (mother of Himetataraisuzu-hime according to the ''Kojiki''), [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshirohime]] (mother of Himetataraisuzu-hime according to the ''Kojiki'') [[Tamakushi-hime|Tamayori-hime]], [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] (father of Himetataraisukehime according to the ''Nihon Shoki'') are enshrined here.<ref>『日本歴史地名大系30奈良県の地名』p435-436「狭井神社」</ref>

She is also worshipped at Tsumori Jingu Shrine ({{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} {{ill|Kamimashiki District, Kumamoto|en|Kamimashiki District, Kumamoto|qid=Q1132187|lt=Kamimashiki District|1=Kamimashiki District, Kumamoto}} {{ill|Mashiki, Kumamoto|en|Mashiki, Kumamoto|qid=Q1206076|lt=Mashiki Town|1=Mashiki, Kumamoto}}) and {{ill|Kosa Shrine|ja|甲佐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosa Shrine|1=Kōsa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11577666}} ({{ill|Kōsa, Kumamoto|en|Kōsa, Kumamoto|qid=Q1184749|lt=Kosa Town|1=Kōsa, Kumamoto}}).<ref>『日本の神仏の辞典』p1094「ひめたたらいすずひめのみこと」</ref>

==Literature==

* "Mythological Hime Tachi: Another Kojiki", {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|en|Sankei Shimbun|qid=Q1197261|lt=Sankei Shimbun|1=Sankei Shimbun}}, Sankei Shimbun, 2018, ISBN 978-4-8191-1336-6
* "Nihon no Kami Yomiwake Jiten" (An Encyclopedia of Japanese Gods), Kenji Kawaguchi/editor, Kashiwa Shobo, 1999, 2009 (9th printing), ISBN 4-7601-1824-1
* "Kojiki to Nihon no Kami ga Kunderu Hon" (The Book of Ancient Matters and Understanding Japanese Gods), Kunihiro Yoshida, Gakken Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-05-406340-2
* "Illustrated Chronicle of the Emperors of the Rekishi", Edited by Masao Mitobe, Kazuo Higo, Shizuko Akagi, Shigetaka Fukuchi, {{ill|Akita Shoten|en|Akita Shoten|qid=Q419352|lt=Akita Shoten|1=Akita Shoten}}, 1989, ISBN 4-253-00297-8
* "Genealogical Compilation", New Edition, Vol. 1, Upper Section, Divine Emperors (1), edited by Yotohiko Iwasawa, Meisho Shuppan, 1996, ISBN 4-626-01541-7
* "A Genealogical Directory of the Empresses of the Rekishi Era" (Bessatsu Rekishi Yomibon 24, Vol. 27, No. 29, 618), Minoru Sato (ed.), Shinninjin Oraisha, 2002
* "Nihonjinmei Daijiten (Shin-Sen Otona-mei Jiten)" Vol.5, Kunihiko Shimonaka/editor, Heibonsha, 1938, 1979 (reprint edition)
* "Nihon Josei Jinmei Jiten (Dictionary of Japanese Women's Biographies), Popular Edition", edited by Noboru Haga, Yasuko Ichibanghase, Kuni Nakajima, Koichi Soda, Japan Book Center, 1998, ISBN 4-8205-7881-2
* "Dictionary of Japanese Historical Personal Names", Nichigai Associates, 1999, ISBN 4-8169-1527-3
* "Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clans and Personal Names, Popular Edition", Taro Sakamoto and Kunio Hirano, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1990, 2010 (Popular Edition, 1st Edition), ISBN 978-4-642-01458-8
* "Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 30 Nara-ken no Chimei" (Japanese Historical Chimei Compendium 30), Heibonsha, 1981.
* "Kadokawa Japanese Dictionary of Geographical Names 29: Nara Prefecture", Kadokawa Japanese Dictionary of Geographical Names Compilation Committee, {{ill|Rizō Takeuchi|en|Rizō Takeuchi|qid=Q4262779|lt=Rizo Takeuchi|1=Rizō Takeuchi}}, editor, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|en|Kadokawa Shoten|qid=Q1054765|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}, 1990, ISBN 4-04-001290-9
